Hey, JB in the house time for part two of a series of weekly intensives on the actual syntax, the overall straight line from start to finish. It's going to be an awesome training. I've been thinking about it all morning in terms of, you know, what could I say to you today that's sort of unique that I've never said before that could give you even deeper insight into your ability to use the straight line system practically, right? Because at the end of the day, that's what this all comes down to is the ability to take what you learn that's kind of theoretical, formulaic, right? And then actually apply it in the real world. And for lack of a bit of term, I was thinking, you know, what is it really about the straight line that makes it work so well? And I think there's something that's kind of weird, right? It's about, you know, you ever hear this expression, you'll line a shit, you know, you know, that guy's got some line of shit. Someone's got a line of shit that is fucking person who just fucking put what a line of shit they have. Now that's not exactly a positive, okay? He really knows how to make himself, you know, his thoughts and ideas known. But in a positive way, where like, it's genuine, it's actually authentic, right? And I'm driving out here is that there's some element of the straight line system at the end of the day, what it allows you to do is just fucking be awesome sound awesome, like you sound fucking awesome, you know what you're talking about, you know, your line of shit is fucking right on point here. And I want you to start thinking of the straight line system, not in a negative way, but just in that one of the things that you must learn how to do as part of mastering the straight line is just fucking sounding great, sounding like you know what you're talking about. You know, there's two types of people in this world. So there's those that know what they're talking about, and those that don't. Right. But actually, it's more complicated because there's lots of people out there who don't know what they're fucking talking about, but sound like they do. And then there are those who really know what it's about just sound like fucking idiots, you wouldn't understand what they're saying like guys not next. In other words, at the end of the day in the sales world, it's not what you know that carries the day. It's how you sound. I'm sorry to say you could be the smartest, most educated person. You can know more about your product. You have the best product in the world. You could have the ability to give great customer service. It has every feature, benefit. It's just awesome. But when you're explaining it to someone, it doesn't come off that way, then it's just simply not. You essentially detract. You essentially, there's a sliver or percentage of whatever you're selling that gets knocked off because you don't know how to explain it. The idea with the straight line is, and the reason I'm going through this now is because the back half of the straight line is really a lot about that, by the way, it's not about overcoming objections. Overcoming objections is the easy part of it. The point is, is that what you're doing in the back half of the sale, and I'll get things into context in a few minutes here, is that you're just making it sound so fucking good. It's irresistible. Your goal as a salesman, as a sales person, as an influencer, is to make what you're selling sound irresistible without lying, without sounding inauthentic. You just say, it's fucking great. And then you're damn like that's fucking good. It's about tying your words together in a certain way that just sounds good because I don't care how good your product is. If when you're done explaining it to someone else, if they don't get that same energy that that's fucking, that wasn't transferred, you're screwed. They're not going to buy from you. So what does that mean in terms of the straight line? Well, number one, when I was thinking about when I went back to the early days of Stratton, right? And what was that it really allowed me to take all these young kids that had been struggling so bad before I invented the straight line. Now remember, I had another system of sales I was teaching before the straight line, and it was great, right? It was really good. Didn't have a name, but it was powerful. And I would teach every morning, I would give a training session every afternoon, and I had these 12 kids and they were calling average moms and pops. And it worked well. It was only when I tried to transition from having these 12 kids call moms and pops to calling the richest 1% of Americans, that was when everything broke down. It's like it's like an engineering situation with, you know, catastrophic failure, right? The bridge seems like it's operating okay, right? There's, you know, 55 cars, you know, there's, you know, three trucks and 20 mile an hour winds, and it's all perfect. Yeah, the bridge works. And then one day the same thing, but the winds are 22 miles an hour and the bridge, oh, fuck, oops, I guess it wasn't as strong as we thought. Like you don't know how good a system is until you stress it to the point of fracture. You get it? It's true with anything. Everything seems like, yeah, it works really well until it doesn't work anymore, right? Well, what happened with my old system of selling was that I thought it was great. The kids were getting really good results calling average moms and pops, but it broke down when they were faced with this really tough sale, calling rich people and was smart, educated, tough, used to being in control and selling them a lot of five dollars. In other words, the straight line system is these different levels of learning, which I've spoken about in the past, about, you know, when you're unconsciously incompetent with the straight line, you don't even know it exists. You know, nothing about anything you suck at selling. You can't sell you any of a paper bag, basically, all right? And that's the lowest level of learning. But then you heard something about the straight line. Maybe you saw the movie, The Wolf of Wall Street, you saw the scenes of how great radio sounding, maybe you saw a video of me selling live or someone told you about it, and you become aware, you know, wow, this is, this is way out there of communication that can really enhance my ability to close. Maybe it's on a money, but we'd be a more effective person, live a more empowering life. You find out about that. So now your awareness is raised. You can't do anything about it. You don't know the system, but you now become what's called consciously incompetent, right? Consciously, you're consciously aware, but you're still incompetent. But then because of that awareness now, what do you do? You take action and you start to learn. You enroll in this program. You start watching videos. You practice, you write scripts, you get mentored, you get tutored, right? And slowly but surely, you actually start learning the system and you get to this point where you're called consciously competent, meaning you now can use the system. You've studied enough that you can actually apply it in the real world, but it takes all your focus, your conscious awareness. You have to really think about it while you're doing it. Because if you, just if you, if your mind gets straight to another area, you're like, oh, I can't do two things at once, right? But that's being consciously competent. It's like you can do it. You get decent results. Doesn't look that pretty. It's kind of clunky and awkward. But then of course, what happens is in the real world is we practice, we repeat, we practice, we rinse and repeat. So we drill, train, drill, train. And before we know that ability switches to our unconscious mind, becomes a pattern of behavior. And all of a sudden we start sounding elegant. We sound awesome. And when you get to that level, which is now called unconscious competence, you're unconsciously competent. You don't even have to think about it to do it. And all the things we do well, by the way, we do really well, we do with unconscious competence, tie our shoelaces, drive up hard, fly a helicopter, play a sport well, you do it when you sit there and swing the back and try to hit a ball or shoot a bat. You're really good. You're not saying, I gotta make sure I, I aim my eyes and line up and do four pounds of practice. I can make sure I gotta keep my wrist straight. You don't think about that. You think Kobe Bryant or Michael Jordan was one of the position of his wrist when he shot. No, your unconscious mind handles everything. That's how it is. Same thing goes in selling. Right. So the way we get to that super high level of learning is by becoming unconsciously competent. Right. So when you're unconsciously competent, you're going to exit this intelligence gathering phase, knowing everything you need to know, being in super tight rapport, knowing where their pain is. You execute your incredibly powerful yet simple straight line transition, which is where you say, you know, Jim, Jill, John, you know, based on everything you just said to me, this program or this prog is a perfect if you're here's what I recommend you. Jim, based on what you just said, here's my recommendation. Here's my prognosis, right. And then that's called your straight line transition. Simple to the point. We then execute a presentation, our straight line presentation, where we frame our ideas. We don't front load them frame them, meaning you don't say, okay, my practice got 26 benefits. I'm going to list all 26 in the best order here. And I'm going to make sure I tell all my great stories and metaphors. Because I just, because I want to hear my thing. I know Jordan told me this is about certainty. Right. So I have to make sure that if they're not really certain about my product, both logically and emotionally, and if they don't trust and connect with me, and if they don't know about the company, they're not going to buy. And I don't want to get hit with the first objection. I hate objections. So I better really make sure I make a very complete presentation here, because if I leave anything out, I'm going to get hit with an objection. I got to make sure I leave nothing out here. So I can then essentially give this guy every fucking thing he needs to know, make it so fucking compelling. He's like, God, Jesus, Zach, I'm done. Or I might as well just say yes. Why say, why do they front load? Why do they delay asking for the order? Why do they say all this stupid shit instead of just framing and then saying, here's what it is. It's like, that's the idiocy of consultative sales. It's not how the world works. Because that's how human beings are. The point was, is that what I was saying to the brokers is, listen, okay, I don't give a shit what bullshit objection they're hitting with you in. Okay. You're not going to speak to this person again. Even they're going to buy from you right now, where you have no shot of closing them again, period and the story. So there is a continuum based on the industry you're in, based on the product you're selling, based on the type of sale cycle you're involved in, that sometimes callbacks do work and they're called for. Okay. So this is not black and white. I don't want you to think it's all black and white. If you were, if you buy now it's fucking over. But on Wall Street, let me just tell you flat out the way it was when you were selling to a super rich person. It's so insanely hard to get them on the phone twice. Do people ever call back and buy? Can I, is there some wiggle room? Seriously, let's work back. I'm going to be very practical because I'm just watching the bar today. I'm going to be very practical with you. In other words, let's get real with each other here. No bullshit, right? We're paying you, paying good money for this. For me to say to you, like, guys, I want you to be kind and gentle on the phone to people and let them off and hopefully they'll, if you're an industry where no one ever buys, they don't, after the first time, you have to accept that fact and be a bit tougher and more backbone. Conversely, you're in an industry that really actually, and there are many that work more on, it might, you get to a certain point, then you call back. They're easy to get in touch with the second time. A lot of us do it with how easy are they to get in touch with the second time. All right. And sometimes it takes three calls, whatever it may be. The point is, is that, you know, if you're in a situation where the numbers, the historical say that you got one shot to close the deal or you're screwed, then dude, you got, and that's the reality. You got to close the deal now, bottom line. And what I don't want you to, and what happens is, is that for novice salespeople, or people who are struggling, is they get so upset about that, is that they're so concerned because they know, they know when they're hot or hot, they're on no callbacks. If I don't get the guy now. So they, what they do is they shoot themselves in the foot because they're scared to ask for the order for the first time. Because once they ask for the order, they get that first objection, they feel like they're screwed. So they end up front loading as a result of that, and they screw themselves even more. So I want you to just fucking suck it up, suck it fucking up. Okay. No matter what industry you're in, whether they never call back, whether it's a, whether it's either they call them the first call when you first walk in the door, or you get a couple of shots, I don't care. I want you to take a deep fucking breath and stick to the protocols of straight line presentations, which are, they don't get front loaded. You frame your key benefits, you make an awesome case, and then you ask for the order for the first time, and you shut up, and let the person hit you with the first objection. And that's where the sale begins. Now, obviously you're selling them from the first moment you open your mouth, but you're the point in this context. I mean, and that's where it truly begins.
